title: Pluricontinentalism

infobox.value: ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Pluricontinentalismo*) was a [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitical "Geopolitical") concept framing [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") as a [transcontinental country](/wiki/List_of_former_transcontinental_countries "List of former transcontinental countries") and a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [nation-state](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") made up of both [continental Portugal](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal") and its [overseas provinces](/wiki/Overseas_province "Overseas province"). Tracing its roots to as early as the 14th century, pluricontinentalism was later promoted under the *[Estado Novo](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)")* regime as a last attempt to justify retaining its remaining colonies. It presented Portugal not as a [colonial empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") but as a single nation-state spanning multiple continents, giving rise to the term.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Macqueen, Norrie (1999). ["Portugal's First Domino: 'Pluricontinentalism' and Colonial War in Guinea-Bissau, 1963-1974"](https://www.jstor.org/stable/20081702). *Contemporary European History*. **8** (2): 209–230. [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") [0960-7773](https://search.worldcat.org/issn/0960-7773).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Historical Portuguese colonial unity concept

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Portuguese_empire_1800.png/250px-Portuguese_empire_1800.png)](/wiki/File:Portuguese_empire_1800.png)

[United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil, and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil,_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil, and the Algarves") and its colonies, 1800.

**Pluricontinentalism** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Pluricontinentalismo*) was a [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitical "Geopolitical") concept framing [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") as a [transcontinental country](/wiki/List_of_former_transcontinental_countries "List of former transcontinental countries") and a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [nation-state](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") made up of both [continental Portugal](/wiki/Continental_Portugal "Continental Portugal") and its [overseas provinces](/wiki/Overseas_province "Overseas province"). Tracing its roots to as early as the 14th century, pluricontinentalism was later promoted under the *[Estado Novo](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)")* regime as a last attempt to justify retaining its remaining colonies. It presented Portugal not as a [colonial empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") but as a single nation-state spanning multiple continents, giving rise to the term.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pluricontinentalism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Through this concept, Portugal's overseas possessions were regarded as integral to its national identity. The first instance of Portugal being a pluricontinental country occurred during the reign of [Maria I of Portugal](/wiki/Maria_I_of_Portugal "Maria I of Portugal"), with the establishment of the [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves"), when the royal court resided in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") functioned as the national capital. The notion of pluricontinentalism collapsed after the [Carnation Revolution](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution") in 1974, leading to the independence of the remaining colonies. The [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") formally came to an end in 1999 with the [Handover of Macau](/wiki/Handover_of_Macau "Handover of Macau").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
